research and question (no code edits):
in the outline-decorations experiments (see @docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md and related docs), we have researched and discarded the approach with per-block editor tree approach. but then in experiment 5a we have discovered that
> Obsidian renders an actively-edited table cell in Live Preview as its own separate, independent CM6 EditorView, mounted inside the outer table widget’s own DOM

aren't these two things related? I don't quite understand the internals and maybe these are completely different things, but it sounds to me that the way Obsidian provides tables WYSIWIG live-preview editing experience is by "drawing" the actual table cells and making each of them an independent editor view. why can't we do the same thing with the outline nodes tree? have a structural tree that looks like a tree and behaves like one, and only when user clicks into one of the nodes, they interact with the embedded editor view for that node's content. 

please explain it in simple terms. 

also, I think one of the assumptions about the researched obsidian-continuous-journal plugin (that I've experimented with in parallel) was that it would require usage of some private APIs and it disqualifies the whole approach. but I actually submitted a draft of the plugin to the registry to run a scan and it hasn't flagged any major issues, so maybe the assumption about that approach wasn't correct and could be revisited (if relevant)
I'll read the related docs first — the Experiment 5 write-up (where the nested-editor discovery lives) and the postmortem / research docs on the per-block editor tree approach.

280	having the setting's own setter toggle outline mode off then immediately back
281	on (via the registry directly, not the user-facing command) — guaranteeing two
282	genuinely different decoration outputs (none vs. the real thing) that CM6
283	always detects, reliably re-triggering `docViewUpdate` twice. This also
284	retroactively fixed the identical latent gap in the earlier placement setting
285	(round 2), which no test happened to exercise against a table-only note.
286	
287	**Follow-up round 4: a genuine architectural bug, found via a flaky test —
288	decorations leaking into Obsidian's own nested per-cell editors.** While
289	verifying the visibility setting's e2e coverage, one specific test (visibility
290	`'headings-and-paragraphs'` against a table) failed intermittently — first
291	misdiagnosed as an async timing race in the table widget's own DOM settling (a
292	`browser.pause()` was gambling against however long that took) and "fixed"
293	with a poll-based wait instead of a fixed sleep, which is a legitimate,
294	permanent improvement in its own right (`waitForContentChildCount` in
295	`e2e/helpers.ts`, mirroring the existing `waitForNotice` pattern) but did *not*
296	fix the underlying test — it kept failing deterministically once actual
297	machine load (which had been intermittently masking/unmasking it across runs)
298	settled down enough to reproduce reliably.
299	
300	Root-caused by tracing the stray marker element's full DOM ancestry: Obsidian
301	renders an actively-edited table cell (cursor inside it) as its own separate,
302	independent CM6 `EditorView`, mounted inside the outer table widget's own DOM
303	(`.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` → `.table-wrapper` → `<table>` → `<tr>` →
304	`<th>`/`<td>` → `.table-cell-wrapper` → a whole nested `.cm-editor`). This
305	plugin's `registerEditorExtension` (`main.ts`) applies its decorations to
306	*every* CM6 instance Obsidian creates app-wide — this nested one included — and
307	that nested editor's own "document" is just the cell's raw text (e.g. a single
308	word), which `decorate()`/`parse()` classifies as a plain paragraph (the
309	default block kind for a bare line with no special syntax) — not an atom, so
310	under `'all'`/`'headings-and-paragraphs'` visibility it becomes marker-eligible
311	and picks up both a stray marker icon *and* depth-based padding/margin exactly
312	like a real top-level line, visibly corrupting the cell being edited. This is a
313	real, live, user-facing bug, not just a test artifact — confirmed independently
314	by the author noticing it in their own use before this was reported back.
315	
316	The state-only "is this note in outline mode" gate (`editorInfoField`, already
317	used throughout `decorations.ts` as a reliable gate everywhere else) cannot
318	distinguish the nested editor from the real top-level one — confirmed live via
319	`EditorView.findFromDOM()` on the nested instance that its own `editorInfoField`
320	resolves to the exact *same* outer `MarkdownView` object as the real note. Only
321	DOM ancestry can tell them apart: a real top-level note's own `.cm-editor` is
322	never itself nested inside a `.cm-embed-block` (those are its own descendants,
323	never its ancestors). Since `StateField.create`/`update` have no view/DOM
324	access at all, fixing this required moving both decoration `StateField`s
325	(`computeDecorations`/`computeMarkers`) to `ViewPlugin`s (which do have
326	`view.dom`), gating each — and `MarginCompensation`, already a ViewPlugin — on
327	a shared `isNestedEditor(view)` check (`view.dom.closest('.cm-embed-block') !==
328	null`). Confirmed fixed via the same DOM-ancestry trace, and via two
329	consecutive clean e2e runs of the affected test (previously flaky, now
330	deterministic) plus the full 8-spec-file suite (58 tests) twice with no
331	regressions.
332	
333	**Code cost of rounds 2-4 combined**: ~20 lines in `decorate.ts`
334	(`hasChildren`), ~15 lines in `mode-registry.ts` (`MarkerVisibility` type +
335	setting), ~15 lines in `main.ts` (setter + the off/on `forceRedraw` toggle +
336	settings dropdown), net ~+85 lines in `decorations.ts` after removing the
337	placement-variant machinery (round 2) and adding the nested-editor guard (round
338	4: two new small `ViewPlugin` wrapper classes replacing two `StateField`s, plus
339	`isNestedEditor`/`shouldShowMarker`).
340

90	- **Grammar**: high-precedence keymaps for Enter/Shift+Enter/Tab/moves (proven).
91	- **Node UX**: decorations for bullets/handles/fold chevrons; block-select highlight via line
92	  decorations; zoom via replace-decorations + breadcrumbs (proven by obsidian-zoom).
93	- **Scope control**: extensions are registered globally but no-op unless the file is an outline
94	  document (frontmatter flag / folder / global mode — open question), checked via metadataCache.
95	
96	*Pros*: 100% public API; perfect-scorecard compatible; mobile works; degrades gracefully (files
97	are plain markdown lists everywhere else); interops with the whole ecosystem (backlinks, graph,
98	search, sync, other plugins). *Cons*: we inherit markdown-view constraints — the DOM is CM6's
99	line-based DOM, so some pixel-perfect block UI (e.g., Notion-style hover menus per block) takes
100	more creativity; "hard" enforcement is a filter over a text buffer, which demands a rigorous,
101	well-tested invariant layer (this is exactly the pure tree-ops + property-test discipline from
102	the TUI prototype).
103	
104	### Option B — Custom view: parse md → own block editor (Kanban/Excalidraw pattern)
105	
106	A `TextFileView` renders the outline as a fully custom (e.g., per-node contenteditable/CM)
107	block UI; the file stays markdown on disk.
108	
109	The blocker: **there is no public mechanism to open `.md` files in a custom view.**
110	Kanban does it by monkey-patching `WorkspaceLeaf.setViewState` (confirmed in source: intercepts
111	`state.type === 'markdown'`, checks for its frontmatter key, rewrites to its view type) — a
112	private-API hack, fragile, and contrary to this project's bar. A
113	[2025 forum thread](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/custom-viewer-ui-for-certain-markdown-files/108104)
114	asking for an official alternative got no answer from the team. Alternatives all lose something
115	essential: a custom extension (`.outline`) forfeits metadataCache/links/graph/mobile-editor
116	interop; "open via command" per file is UX friction and re-breaks on every workspace restore.
117	
118	*Nuance discovered later (2026-07-13, from the
119	[workflowy-style-outline](https://github.com/springrain1/workflowy-style-outline) case study —
120	see the plugin-landscape doc)*: the monkey-patch is only needed for **automatic** association.
121	An **explicit, user-invoked toggle** — a command/menu item calling
122	`leaf.setViewState({type: OUR_VIEW})` on the active leaf — is plain public API, and workspace
123	persistence keeps a toggled leaf in that view across restarts. So a scorecard-clean custom view
124	is *reachable*; what remains against Option B is everything else that project demonstrates:
125	rebuilding the editor (per-block textareas, IME/mobile/undo/save-queue re-implementations,
126	an "isolation layer" to avoid breaking native Obsidian) and, in their case, a lossy
127	md↔outline conversion — the anti-example for our isomorphism requirement.
128	
129	*Verdict*: keep as a possible far-future layer (the explicit-toggle variant lowers the API
130	barrier but not the engineering cost); not a foundation compatible with today's goals.
131	
132	### Option C — Hybrid (A + owned side surfaces)
133	
134	Option A for editing, plus our own `registerView` panes where we fully control rendering:
135	**structured backlinks pane** (tree context from `listItems`, editable via `Vault.process` or
136	embedded editors), zoom breadcrumb header, optional outline-wide search/filter view. All public.
137	This captures most of what Option B wanted anyway, without the takeover problem.
138	
139	## Feasibility scorecard per target feature
140	
141	| Target behavior | Public-API path | Confidence |
142	| --- | --- | --- |
143	| Enter/Tab/move/merge grammar | CM6 keymaps (proven in prod) | High |
144	| Structure enforcement | `transactionFilter` invariant layer | High (novel engineering, proven primitives) |
145	| Node selection model | transactionFilter + decorations (pro-outliner v1 exists) | High |
146	| Fold + persistence | native folds; storage decision open (plugin data vs in-file) | High |
147	| Zoom + breadcrumbs | replace-decorations (proven: obsidian-zoom) | High |
148	| Drag & drop | CM6 drop + widgets (proven: obsidian-outliner) | High |
149	| Multiline nodes | md continuation lines + grammar keymaps | Medium-high (careful spec needed) |
150	| Block refs/embeds | native `^id` / `![[#^id]]` | High (core does the heavy lifting) |
151	| Mirrors (synced blocks) | native embeds first; true editable mirrors later | Medium |
152	| Structured backlinks pane (read) | own view + `resolvedLinks` + `listItems` | High |
153	| Editable backlinks | + `Vault.process`/embedded editor per block | Medium |
154	| Mobile | all of the above is CM6/DOM | High (test discipline) |
155	| Perfect scorecard | eslint-plugin from day 1; no `any`-casts into internals | High under Option A |
156	
157	Two honesty notes: (a) `getBacklinksForFile` is **not** public — the public path is
158	`resolvedLinks` + own reverse index (the [backlink-cache](https://github.com/mnaoumov/obsidian-backlink-cache)
159	plugin shows the pattern); (b) even obsidian-outliner reaches for `(editor as any).cm` — we can
160	avoid it almost everywhere by living inside registered extensions (a `ViewPlugin` receives its
161	`EditorView` directly), which should be an explicit engineering rule.
162	
163	## Addendum: the universal isomorphic outline view (post-alignment)
164	
165	The 2026-07-12 alignment (see [04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md), Q2) widened the
166	document model from "lists" to the **full markdown block tree** — headings, paragraphs, code
167	fences, quotes, tables *and* list items are all nodes; the outline is a toggleable editor UI
168	over any note, with a lossless (isomorphic) round-trip. Feasibility notes for that vision:
169

content.text:
Good question — the two things are genuinely related, and you've spotted the right connection. Let me explain what Obsidian is actually doing with table cells, why that trick doesn't transfer to a whole outline the way it might seem, and then address the continuous-journal point (where the docs actually predicted your scan result).

## Yes, the table cell is a mini per-block editor — done by Obsidian, from the inside

What we found in Experiment 5a ([10-experiment-5-block-markers.md:300](docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md:300)) is exactly the pattern you describe: the table is a "drawn" structural widget, and the one cell you're editing gets a real, live editor mounted inside it. So conceptually, yes — Obsidian's table editing *is* a per-block editor tree, in miniature. The reason we can't lift that pattern for outline nodes comes down to who is doing it and at what scale:

**1. Obsidian does it with its own internals, which are not offered to plugins.** That nested cell editor isn't a bare CodeMirror instance — it's a full Obsidian markdown editor (Live Preview rendering, link autocomplete, the app's whole extension stack) that Obsidian constructs using its private editor-factory machinery, and Obsidian's own private code keeps the cell's tiny document synced with the real file underneath. A plugin can create raw CM6 `EditorView`s (that part is public), but what you get is a plain text box — not an Obsidian editor. To make each outline node feel like Obsidian, you'd have to either reach into the private factory or rebuild Live Preview yourself. That's the same wall as the custom-view Option B in [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:104](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:104).

**2. Obsidian only ever does it for *one* cell at a time.** This is the part that makes their version tractable and ours not. The nested editor exists only while your cursor is in that cell; click away and it's torn down and replaced by static rendered HTML. So at any moment there is exactly one small live editor, over one small, self-contained slice of text. The "N editors bound to overlapping ranges of one shared file, kept consistent as any one of them edits" problem — the unsolved problem named in [07-decoration-experiments-plan.md:147](docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md:147) — simply never arises for them, because N=1 by design. An outline view wants the whole tree visible and clickable at once, and outline nodes *nest* (a parent's text range contains its children's), which is strictly harder than a grid of disjoint cells.

**3. The table is an atomic island; the outline is the whole document.** The outer editor treats the entire table as one opaque replacement widget — a single "atom" it doesn't try to render as text. That's why there's no double-ownership fight: the outer view shows the widget, the inner view owns the text. To do this for an outline you'd have to widget-replace essentially *every line of the document*, leaving the outer CM6 view as an empty shell whose only job is hosting widgets. At that point you haven't extended Obsidian's editor — you've replaced it, which is precisely the custom-view path the project already rejected on engineering-cost grounds (IME, mobile, undo, save queues — the workflowy-style-outline anti-example in [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:118](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:118)).

**4. Our own bug report is evidence of how non-public this machinery is.** The nested cell editor is invisible to every public API: its `editorInfoField` resolves to the *same* `MarkdownView` as the real editor, and only DOM-ancestry sniffing (`closest('.cm-embed-block')`) can tell them apart. Even the Lapel plugin's Obsidian-team reviewer said there's "no official way to not load an editor extension inside table cells" ([10-experiment-5-block-markers.md:848](docs/research/10-experiment-5-block-markers.md:848)). If Obsidian doesn't even expose "you are inside a nested editor" as a signal, it certainly doesn't expose "here's how to make your own."

So the simple summary: **Obsidian's table trick proves the architecture works when the host app does it — one editor at a time, over an atomic island, with private glue. It doesn't prove a plugin can do it, at document scale, over nested ranges, with public glue.** The discovery and the rejection are consistent, not contradictory.

## The continuous-journal scan result: expected, and it doesn't overturn the reasoning — but it does sharpen one question

Your clean registry scan actually confirms something the research already recorded rather than contradicting it: the automated review explicitly does **not** check for monkey-patching or private-API access. That's stated in both [02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md:141](docs/research/02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md:141) ("Notably absent: any check for monkey-patching or private-API access — those surface in human review") and [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:26](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:26). So "the scanner didn't flag it" was the predicted outcome even for a plugin that leans on private APIs — it's evidence about the scanner, not about the plugin.

The per-block approach was shelved on three grounds, and the scan only touches the weakest one:

1. **Registry acceptance** — your experiment shows the automated gate is passable; human review might still push back on monkey-patching (it's visible in source), but this barrier is evidently lower than a hard "disqualified."
2. **The project's own self-imposed bar** — "100% public API, no hacks" was a *decision* (Q1), made for fragility reasons: private surfaces break silently across Obsidian updates, especially on mobile. The doc explicitly leaves the door open: "revisit only if that bar itself is ever renegotiated" ([07-decoration-experiments-plan.md:154](docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md:154)). Your scan result is a legitimate input to renegotiating it — but it's a values question (how much update-fragility you'll accept), not a facts question the scan settles.
3. **The unsolved engineering problem** — N live editors over nested ranges of one file, kept consistent. This one is untouched by the scan, and it's the load-bearing objection. Even with the API bar dropped to zero, nobody in the ecosystem has demonstrated this working (continuous-journal included — its trick is grafting whole *separate files'* editors into one pane, which is a much easier problem than splitting *one* file across many editors).

So my honest read: the assumption worth revising is narrow — "private APIs → automatic registry disqualification" was indeed too strong, and your experiment proves it. But that was never the decisive argument. The decisive arguments (fragility across updates, and the one-file/many-editors consistency problem that even Obsidian sidesteps by only ever mounting one nested editor at a time) stand exactly as before. If you ever want to revisit, the productive framing isn't "can we pass the scan" but "are we willing to own breakage on Obsidian updates, and do we have an answer to multi-editor sync that Obsidian itself avoided needing."

I want to clarify this a bit further. First of all, I didn't suggest that we build a whole tree of nested editor views. I meant a trick more similar to the way tables are handled, where a structural nodes tree is rendered visually, but we insert an editor view only on the node of the tree, only on the single node of the tree, on one block that is currently selected and that the user is interacting with. The same way as the table, only one editor view is active at a time, and the rest of the tree is just a structure with rendered blocks of content. 

I understand that this is still an undocumented and private API, and that is an actual problem, but at least the concern about unreasonable overhead of building a whole tree of embedded editors is not really a reason to reject this approach. What I am still not clear about is the distinction between blocks of content that can be viewed in their separate editor views and synced back to the underlying file and the lines of text. I understand that with our current approach, we work on the lines. We parse the lines, and each line is a separate node in the DOM. We group them and handle the indentation per grouped set of lines. Why can't we group the lines at the level of the DOM?

Your clarification changes the picture meaningfully, so let me first concede the point, then answer the actual question — why lines can't just be grouped in the DOM — because that's the crux, and it has a precise technical answer.

## You're right: one-editor-at-a-time removes the sync objection

The doc's "N editors over overlapping ranges" objection was aimed at a persistent tree of live editors. Your design — static rendered tree, one live editor materialized under the focused node, torn down on blur — is exactly the table pattern, and it has the same property that makes tables tractable: at any moment there's one editor over one small range, so the multi-editor consistency problem never exists. That objection doesn't apply to your variant. What remains are different problems, and they live in your second question.

## Why you can't group lines in the DOM: the DOM isn't ours

The mental model to install is: **CM6's content DOM is not a document you build — it's a projection CM6 owns and continuously rewrites.** The contract is rigid: `.cm-content` contains a flat list of `.cm-line` elements, one per visible line, nothing in between. CM6 depends on that flatness for everything positional: mapping a mouse click to a text offset, placing the cursor rectangle, drawing selections, handling IME composition. On every update it diffs and patches this DOM itself — so even if you reached in and wrapped five lines in a `<div class="node">`, CM6's next reconciliation pass would move, recreate, or re-parent those lines and shred your wrapper. It's the same reason you can't manually edit React's rendered output: you'd be fighting the owner of the DOM.

The *sanctioned* ways to alter what's rendered are decorations, and there are only three shapes:

- **Line decorations** — add attributes/classes to a line element (what we do now: indentation, markers).
- **Widget decorations** — insert an inert element at a point.
- **Replace decorations** — hide a range entirely and optionally show one opaque widget in its place.

Notice what's missing: there is **no decoration type that wraps a range of lines in a shared parent element while keeping them live lines**. That's not an oversight; it would break the flat-DOM contract that position-mapping depends on. This is the whole technical gap, stated in one sentence.

## So "grouping" is possible — but it's a one-way door

You *can* group lines: that's a replace decoration over the block's multi-line range. But look at what it means. CM6 is told "this range is no longer text; here's one black box." The lines vanish from the DOM; inside the widget you can build any DOM you want — a table, a tree node card, anything. The price is that the black box is **inert to the editor**: no cursor can enter it, no selection can cross it, find-in-note can't highlight inside it, spellcheck and other plugins' decorations don't reach it, because to CM6 it's a single atomic character.

**That's precisely why the table needs the nested editor.** It's not an optimization — it's a forced move. Obsidian widget-replaced the table range to get free-form DOM, which made the text uneditable, so it must mount a fresh editor inside the widget the moment you click a cell, and privately sync it back. The nested editor is the *repair* for what replacement broke.

So the distinction you asked about — "blocks in editor views" vs "lines of text" — is really a binary each range must choose:

| | Live lines | Replaced widget |
|---|---|---|
| DOM shape | flat `.cm-line`s, un-groupable | anything you want |
| Editing, cursor, selection | native, free | dead — needs a grafted editor |
| Search/vim/other plugins | all work | all blind to it |

There is no middle state. Grouped-but-still-live doesn't exist in CM6.

## The interesting consequence for your proposal

Here's the part worth sitting with: your design is "everything is a rendered widget except the focused block." But notice — if the focused block is *not* replaced, it's just… ordinary live lines in the ordinary outer editor. **You don't need a nested editor at all.** Tables need one because a table's raw source (one giant pipe-delimited line) is unusable as an editing surface. An outline node's raw source is exactly the markdown you want the user typing into. So the "one active editor" in your scheme can simply be the outer editor showing the focused block as normal lines, while every *other* block is replace-decorated into a rendered card. No private API for that part — `Decoration.replace` and `MarkdownRenderer.render` are both public.

And here's the reveal: **that architecture already exists — it's Live Preview itself.** "Render blocks as widgets, un-render the one the cursor is in" is literally Obsidian's Live Preview algorithm; it just renders widgets as flat prose instead of a tree. Your proposal amounts to reimplementing Live Preview's render pipeline with tree-shaped DOM around the blocks.

That's not absurd — but the costs are now precisely nameable, and they're the widget-inertness costs, multiplied by "the whole document is widgets": arrow-key travel through atomic widgets is clunky (CM6 hops over them as single characters), selecting text across two blocks is impossible, note-wide search highlighting goes dark, every third-party editor plugin stops seeing your content, and you're re-rendering markdown previews for every block on every structural change. Our Experiment 1 already met the small version of this pain — the `.cm-embed-block` atoms (tables, callouts) that refused line decorations and needed the inline-style `ViewPlugin` workaround — and that was with only a *few* atoms per note.

So the honest summary: your instinct was architecturally sound (it's Live Preview's own trick), the N-editors objection genuinely doesn't apply to it, and the private-API footprint is smaller than the shelved variant — possibly near zero if the focused block stays in the outer editor. The real trade is different and cleaner to state: the current approach keeps 100% of native editing and decorates around it; yours buys free-form tree DOM by making every unfocused block invisible to the editor and everything built on it. That's a legitimate future experiment — "Live-Preview-style block widgetization" — but it competes against a decoration stack that, per experiments 1/2b/5a, is no longer struggling to convey the tree.

okay. I understand it now, I think. and yes, we want the Live-Preview in lines. but my concern here is not about just rendering the tree structure. sure, we achieved that by patching every line with our decorations. this seems to work well for _showing_ the outline. but what about interactions like editing and selection. the first point of our TLDR "manifest" is

> A **true outliner** is defined by one invariant: *the document is a tree of nodes, and every operation — typing, selecting, deleting, moving, pasting — respects node boundaries*. Existing Obsidian plugins (obsidian-outliner et al.) bolt keyboard tricks onto flat text; the invariant is never enforced, so the structure is always one careless selection away from breaking

So I'm curious, how can we achieve the goal of respecting those _structural boundaries_ in every interaction, while our underlying CM model is still flat and split into lines? Aren't we going to end up with the same keyboard tricks? Maybe I'm missing something from our initial feasibility research, so I'd appreciate it if you could clarify this and explain it in simple terms
Let me ground this in what the feasibility research actually claimed before explaining — the key phrase there was "transactionFilter invariant layer," and it's worth checking how much was worked out.

content.text:
You're not missing something — the feasibility research did answer this, but the answer lives in one dense phrase ("`transactionFilter` invariant layer", [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:144](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:144)) that deserves the unpacking you're asking for. Here it is in simple terms.

## The distinction: intercepting inputs vs. intercepting outcomes

What the manifest calls "keyboard tricks" is architecture, not effort: obsidian-outliner works by intercepting *inputs*. It rebinds Enter, Tab, Backspace, and handles each one specially. The fatal property of that approach is that its coverage is an enumerated list — anything not on the list walks straight past it: a mouse drag-selection that grabs half of one node and half of another, a paste, find-and-replace, a vim command, another plugin dispatching an edit, multi-cursor typing. That's exactly why "the structure is always one careless selection away from breaking" — the guards stand at *some* doors, and the document has many doors.

CM6 has a property that changes the game entirely: **every mutation of the document or selection, from every source, is expressed as a transaction, and all transactions pass through one funnel before applying.** Keystroke, mouse, paste, drag-drop, IME, search-replace, another plugin's programmatic edit — there is no side channel; nothing touches the buffer except through `dispatch`. A `transactionFilter` is a checkpoint installed in that funnel: it sees every proposed change *before it happens* and can pass it, rewrite it, or veto it.

The database analogy is the cleanest: keyboard tricks are validation code copy-pasted into every form of an app — miss one form and bad data gets in. A `transactionFilter` is a constraint in the database itself. It doesn't matter which app, which form, which script writes — the constraint holds, because there is no write path around it.

## How that enforces a tree over flat lines

The flat line buffer is just the *storage format*; the tree is a lens over it (our `parse()` already computes it). The invariant to enforce is: **the buffer always parses to a well-formed tree.** With a checkpoint on the only write path, you get that by induction — the document starts valid, and the filter guarantees every accepted transaction *preserves* validity, therefore it's valid forever. Concretely, for each incoming transaction the filter asks: what tree operation does this text change amount to?

- **It's already a valid tree op** (typing inside a node's text) → pass through untouched. The common case costs nothing.
- **It crosses a boundary but has a structural meaning** → *rewrite* it into the structural equivalent: a deletion spanning from mid-node-A to mid-node-B becomes "delete these whole nodes" or gets clamped to the boundary; a paste gets re-indented to a valid depth; a selection that crosses node boundaries gets escalated to whole-node selection (selection is part of the transaction too, so it's normalizable in the same place — pro-outliner ships a v1 of exactly this).
- **It has no valid structural meaning** (a change that would orphan children or corrupt a node header) → veto; the transaction simply never applies.

And here's the reframe that answers "aren't we going to end up with the same keyboard tricks": our keymaps (the Enter/Tab grammar we already have) stop being the enforcement and become mere *ergonomics* — convenient generators of already-valid transactions. The filter underneath is the actual invariant. obsidian-outliner has the first layer with nothing underneath; the whole thesis of this project is that second layer. Same keys, categorically different architecture.

## The honest caveats

The research flagged this as "High confidence, novel engineering" ([03:144](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:144)) and both words matter:

- **The rewrite logic is the hard, novel part.** Mapping an arbitrary text diff to "which tree operation is this?" is the real product — that's why the plan insists on the pure tree-ops + property-test discipline from the TUI prototype ([03:100](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md:100)): the filter should delegate to a pure, exhaustively-tested `text diff → tree op | reject` function, not accumulate ad-hoc cases.
- **It must be fast**, since it runs on every keystroke — hence incremental parsing (the CM6 syntax tree, not a full re-parse).
- **Some genuinely fiddly corners exist**: IME composition mustn't be broken mid-stream by rewrites, and rewritten/vetoed transactions interact with undo history in ways that need care. These are known-solvable (filters are a designed-for CM6 feature, used in production), but they're where the engineering rigor goes.
- **Cursor motion** has its own primitive: `atomicRanges` makes structural markers un-enterable by the caret, so the cursor can't even *land* somewhere that would invite an invalid edit.

So the one-sentence answer to your question: the model being flat lines doesn't force input-level tricks, because CM6 offers a single choke point through which *all* edits flow — enforcement lives there, as a validator/rewriter of proposed changes against the parsed tree, and that is precisely the "invariant layer" the feasibility doc bet the architecture on. It's also worth noting this enforcement question is fully orthogonal to the decoration work we've been doing — decorations make the tree *visible*, the filter makes it *unbreakable*, and neither depends on the other's mechanism.

# outline-keyboard-grammar Specification

## Purpose
Defines the CM6 keyboard grammar that drives structural editing directly from the keyboard
in outline mode: which keys map to which structural operations and node-split behavior, how
the grammar scopes itself to outline-mode files, and the transaction/undo contract each
accepted keypress SHALL satisfy.

## Requirements
### Requirement: Grammar is scoped to outline mode
The keyboard grammar SHALL be registered as a CodeMirror extension via
`registerEditorExtension` and SHALL activate per keypress only when the editor's file has
outline mode enabled (resolved through the public `editorInfoField`). Outside outline
mode every grammar binding SHALL decline the key so editor behavior is byte-for-byte
stock Obsidian.

#### Scenario: Stock behavior off-mode
- **WHEN** Tab is pressed in a list in a note without outline mode
- **THEN** Obsidian's default indent behavior runs, unaffected by the plugin

#### Scenario: Toggle takes effect immediately
- **WHEN** outline mode is toggled while the note is open
- **THEN** the next keypress already follows the new mode, with no editor reload

### Requirement: Structural key bindings
In outline mode, high-precedence bindings SHALL map Tab → indent node, Shift+Tab →
outdent node, Alt+ArrowUp → move node up, Alt+ArrowDown → move node down, targeting the
node at the cursor line. Each accepted operation SHALL dispatch as one CM6 transaction
(annotated with a `userEvent`) forming a single undo step, with the selection placed at
the operation's cursor result. Rejections SHALL show the transient cue and change

content.text:
Here's the picture from the current codebase: the pure tree-ops core exists and is tested ([ops.ts](src/ops.ts): indent/outdent/move/split), the keyboard grammar and decorations are merged, and there is **zero enforcement code** — no `transactionFilter` anywhere in [src/plugin/](src/plugin/). Everything the manifest calls the differentiator (Q4: "the *enforced* core editor — grammar + node selection + structure invariants") still has its third pillar missing, and it's exactly the "High confidence, novel engineering" bet from the feasibility scorecard. So yes — the enforcement layer is the right next spec. Nothing else needs to land first; the prerequisites it needs (a parsed tree per state, node-at-cursor lookup in [locate.ts](src/plugin/locate.ts), pure ops to delegate to) all exist.

But I'd strongly advise against making "editing and selection through transactionFilter" one big proposal. The decoration experiments taught this project its most valuable process lesson — small, isolated, falsifiable slices with explicit kill criteria — and the enforcement layer has exactly the shape that benefits. Here's the staging I'd propose, riskiest-assumption-first:

## Phase A — the observer: prove the choke point is real (smallest possible slice)

A `transactionFilter` that **changes nothing** — it only classifies every transaction (safe within-node edit / boundary-crossing / selection-crossing / programmatic) and records what it sees, wired into the existing `crosscheck` dev tooling. This directly tests the assumptions my previous answer stated as fact but the project has never verified in Obsidian:

- **Does everything really flow through the funnel?** Paste, mouse drag-selection, drag-drop, find-and-replace, spellcheck fixes, other plugins' dispatches. The whole architecture rests on "no side channels" — cheap to verify, catastrophic to discover late.
- **Can we tell user edits from programmatic ones?** Obsidian Sync, external-file reloads, and template plugins dispatch whole-document changes. The filter must wave those through untouched (fighting Sync would corrupt the Q6 interop guarantee), which means relying on `userEvent` annotations — verify they're actually present and trustworthy for each source.
- **What does it cost per keystroke?** The filter needs the tree for the *proposed* new state. Measure whether the existing parse is cheap enough at real note sizes or whether incremental reuse is needed.
- **The nested-editor problem returns in a nastier form.** Our hardest decoration bug — extensions running inside Obsidian's per-table-cell editors — applies here too, but a `transactionFilter` is state-level with *no DOM access*, so the `isNestedEditor()` DOM-ancestry gate that saved the decorations **cannot work** in a filter. This needs a design answer (possibly: the nested cell's mini-document never parses as multi-node, so enforcement degenerates to a no-op there — but that's a hypothesis to test, not assume).
- IME composition transactions — observe what they look like before ever rewriting one.

This phase is almost pure learning per line of code, and any red flag here reshapes everything downstream.

## Phase B — selection enforcement: the first behavior change

Normalize selections, not edits: a selection that crosses a node boundary escalates to whole-node selection (the pro-outliner-v1 pattern). Two reasons this comes before edit rewriting:

1. It's the manifest's literal failure mode — "one careless selection away" — addressed directly and visibly. Easy to demo, easy to e2e-test, low ambiguity about correct behavior.
2. **It shrinks Phase C.** If selections can never straddle a boundary mid-node, then delete/type-over/cut on a selection is *already* boundary-aligned. The remaining edit-enforcement surface reduces to a short list: Backspace at node start, Delete at node end, multi-line paste, dropped text.

## Phase C — edit rewriting: the actual novel engineering

The pure function `(tree, proposedChanges) → pass | rewrite(structuralOp) | veto`, living in `src/` beside [ops.ts](src/ops.ts) under the same property-test discipline, with the filter as a thin adapter. Boundary-crossing deletions become structural deletions, pastes re-indent to valid depth, nothing can orphan children. This is where undo interplay and the "no valid structural meaning → veto" cases get specified. It's the biggest phase, and it's much safer specified after A and B have produced real data about what transactions actually look like in the wild.

## Practical recommendation

Make the next OpenSpec change proposal **Phases A+B together** (working name: something like `outline-selection-enforcement`) — A alone is too thin to be a change, and B is the smallest user-visible slice that proves the whole mechanism; Phase C becomes its own follow-up change informed by A's findings. The spec's requirements should carry the falsifiable framing: each Phase-A question above is a scenario with a concrete pass/fail, in the same spirit as the experiment ground rules that worked so well for decorations.

If that staging matches your intent, say the word and I'll kick off `/openspec-propose` for the A+B change.

Other commands do not take the flag. Hints printed by commands already carry the flag; keep it on follow-ups. Without a store, commands act on the nearest local `openspec/` root.

**Input**: The user's request should include a change name (kebab-case) OR a description of what they want to build.

**Steps**

1. **If no clear input provided, ask what they want to build**

   Use the **AskUserQuestion tool** (open-ended, no preset options) to ask:
   > "What change do you want to work on? Describe what you want to build or fix."

   From their description, derive a kebab-case name (e.g., "add user authentication" → `add-user-auth`).

   **IMPORTANT**: Do NOT proceed without understanding what the user wants to build.

2. **Create the change directory**
   ```bash
   openspec new change "<name>"
   ```
   This creates a scaffolded change in the planning home resolved by the CLI with `.openspec.yaml`.

3. **Get the artifact build order**
   ```bash
   openspec status --change "<name>" --json
   ```
   Parse the JSON to get:
   - `applyRequires`: array of artifact IDs needed before implementation (e.g., `["tasks"]`)
   - `artifacts`: list of all artifacts with their status and dependencies
   - `planningHome`, `changeRoot`, `artifactPaths`, and `actionContext`: path and scope context. Use these instead of assuming repo-local paths.

4. **Create artifacts in sequence until apply-ready**

   Use the **TodoWrite tool** to track progress through the artifacts.

   Loop through artifacts in dependency order (artifacts with no pending dependencies first):

   a. **For each artifact that is `ready` (dependencies satisfied)**:
      - Get instructions:
        ```bash
        openspec instructions <artifact-id> --change "<name>" --json
        ```
      - The instructions JSON includes:
        - `context`: Project background (constraints for you - do NOT include in output)
        - `rules`: Artifact-specific rules (constraints for you - do NOT include in output)
        - `template`: The structure to use for your output file
        - `instruction`: Schema-specific guidance for this artifact type
        - `resolvedOutputPath`: Resolved path or pattern to write the artifact
        - `dependencies`: Completed artifacts to read for context
      - Read any completed dependency files for context
      - Create the artifact file using `template` as the structure and write it to `resolvedOutputPath`
      - Apply `context` and `rules` as constraints - but do NOT copy them into the file
      - Show brief progress: "Created <artifact-id>"

   b. **Continue until all `applyRequires` artifacts are complete**
      - After creating each artifact, re-run `openspec status --change "<name>" --json`
      - Check if every artifact ID in `applyRequires` has `status: "done"` in the artifacts array
      - Stop when all `applyRequires` artifacts are done

   c. **If an artifact requires user input** (unclear context):
      - Use **AskUserQuestion tool** to clarify
      - Then continue with creation

5. **Show final status**
   ```bash
   openspec status --change "<name>"
   ```

**Output**

After completing all artifacts, summarize:
- Change name and location
- List of artifacts created with brief descriptions
- What's ready: "All artifacts created! Ready for implementation."
- Prompt: "Run `/opsx:apply` or ask me to implement to start working on the tasks."

**Artifact Creation Guidelines**

- Follow the `instruction` field from `openspec instructions` for each artifact type
- The schema defines what each artifact should contain - follow it
- Read dependency artifacts for context before creating new ones
- Use `template` as the structure for your output file - fill in its sections
- **IMPORTANT**: `context` and `rules` are constraints for YOU, not content for the file
  - Do NOT copy `<context>`, `<rules>`, `<project_context>` blocks into the artifact
  - These guide what you write, but should never appear in the output

**Guardrails**
- Create ALL artifacts needed for implementation (as defined by schema's `apply.requires`)
- Always read dependency artifacts before creating a new one
- If context is critically unclear, ask the user - but prefer making reasonable decisions to keep momentum
- If a change with that name already exists, ask if user wants to continue it or create a new one
- Verify each artifact file exists after writing before proceeding to next

Defines the structural operations (indent, outdent, moveUp, moveDown) that edit the block
tree from document-tree-mapping: their per-node-kind algebra (heading level-shift vs.
reparent), rejection semantics for inexpressible edits, and the closure/minimal-edit
guarantees that keep every accepted operation's output re-parseable and diff-minimal.

# Proposal: outline-selection-enforcement

## Why

The project's defining invariant — every operation respects node boundaries — has no
enforcement mechanism yet: the grammar keymaps and structural commands only *generate*
valid edits, while every other mutation path (mouse selection, paste, find-and-replace,
other plugins) reaches the document unchecked, which is exactly the "one careless
selection away from breaking" failure the manifest calls out. The architecture bet behind
fixing this — a CM6 `transactionFilter` as a single choke point over all document and
selection changes — was scored "High confidence, novel engineering" in the feasibility
research (docs/research/03) but has never been validated in a real Obsidian editor. This
change builds the smallest slice that both validates the bet with falsifiable checks
(Phase A) and ships the first user-visible enforcement: node-boundary selection
normalization (Phase B).

## What Changes

- **Phase A — transaction classification (observe, never alter document text).** A
  `transactionFilter` registered via `registerEditorExtension` that classifies every
  transaction in outline-mode editors against the parsed tree (within-node edit /
  boundary-crossing edit / selection-only / programmatic-or-remote / composition) and
  exposes the classification to dev instrumentation (extending the existing `crosscheck`
  dialect-drift pattern). It must answer, with concrete pass/fail evidence, the
  choke-point assumptions the enforcement architecture rests on:
  - every mutation path (typing, paste, drag-drop, find-and-replace, spellcheck, other
    plugins' dispatches) flows through the filter;
  - programmatic/remote changes (Obsidian Sync, external reload, `Vault.process`) are
    reliably distinguishable via transaction annotations and are always passed through
    untouched;
  - per-transaction classification cost stays within a keystroke-latency budget at
    real note sizes;
  - behavior inside Obsidian's nested per-table-cell editors is safe by construction or
    explicitly gated (the state-level filter cannot use the DOM-ancestry
    `isNestedEditor` gate the decorations use);
  - IME composition transactions are recognized and never interfered with.
- **Phase B — node-boundary selection enforcement.** Selection changes whose range would
  straddle a node boundary are normalized in the same funnel: the selection escalates to
  cover whole nodes (the pro-outliner-v1 pattern, rebuilt on our universal block tree).
  Cursor placement (empty selections) is never altered. Off-mode notes and pass-through
  transaction classes are byte-for-byte and behavior-identical to stock Obsidian.
- **Explicitly out of scope (Phase C, a later change):** rewriting or vetoing *document
  edits* (boundary-crossing deletions → structural deletions, paste re-indentation,
  orphan prevention). This change never modifies transaction *changes*, only selection.

## Capabilities

### New Capabilities

- `transaction-classification`: the enforcement funnel — a transactionFilter scoped to
  outline mode that classifies every transaction against the parsed tree, its
  pass-through contract for programmatic/remote/composition transactions, its
  performance budget, its nested-editor safety, and its dev-facing observability.
- `node-selection-enforcement`: selection normalization built on the funnel — when and
  how a boundary-crossing selection escalates to whole-node coverage, cursor and
  multi-range semantics, and the stock-behavior guarantees outside outline mode.

### Modified Capabilities

<!-- none: existing specs' requirements are unchanged; new behavior lands as new
     capabilities, and existing gating/e2e patterns are reused, not altered -->

## Impact

- **New code**: a pure classifier module in `src/` (mirroring the `ops.ts`/`dispatch.ts`
  pure-function + property-test discipline: `(tree, transaction facts) → class`), a thin
  CM6 `transactionFilter` adapter plus selection-normalization logic in `src/plugin/`,
  wired in `main.ts` alongside the existing grammar/decoration extensions.
- **Existing code touched**: `main.ts` (register the extension), `crosscheck.ts` or a
  sibling dev-instrumentation surface (classification counters/log), reuse of
  `locate.ts` (node-at-line) and the parse cache the decorations already maintain.
- **Tests**: unit/property tests for the classifier and escalation math; e2e specs
  driving real mutation paths (mouse selection, paste, find-and-replace, sync-like
  programmatic edits, table-cell nested editors) — the Phase A assumption checks become
  permanent regression tests.
- **No file-format, settings-schema, or existing-spec behavior changes.** Off-mode
  notes and all pass-through transaction classes remain byte-identical to stock
  Obsidian, preserving the Q6 interop guarantees.

# Design: outline-selection-enforcement ## Context The enforcement (invariant) layer is the third pillar of the decided v1 core (grammar + node selection + structure invariants, Q4) and the only one with no code behind it. The feasibility research (docs/research/03) bet the architecture on a CM6 `transactionFilter` as a single choke point — every document/selection mutation, from every source, flows through one funnel where it can be classified and normalized. That bet has never been exercised inside a real Obsidian editor, and the decoration experiments taught us that "every CM6 instance app-wide" has surprises (nested per-table-cell editors, Live Preview widget atoms) that state-level mental models miss. This change builds the funnel in two phases within one change: - **Phase A (observe)**: a classifier that inspects every transaction and alters nothing about document text — pure feasibility instrumentation with permanent regression value. - **Phase B (selection)**: the first real enforcement — node-boundary selection escalation — built on the classifier, altering only selection, never changes. Edit rewriting/vetoing (Phase C) is a later change, deliberately specified only after Phase A produces real data about what transactions look like in the wild. Relevant existing machinery: `parse()` (line → tree, byte-lossless), `locate.ts` (`nodeAtLine`), the grammar's `userEvent`-annotated dispatches, the `crosscheck` dev instrumentation pattern (debug setting + console), and e2e helpers that can execute JS inside the app to read plugin-exposed state. ## Goals / Non-Goals **Goals:** - Validate, with falsifiable evidence, the five choke-point assumptions: full mutation-path coverage; reliable programmatic/remote detection; per-transaction cost within a keystroke budget; nested-editor safety without DOM access; IME non-interference. - Ship node-boundary selection enforcement: a non-empty selection can never straddle a node boundary mid-node in outline mode. - Keep the fail-safe direction *permissive*: anything unrecognized passes through untouched. In this change a misclassification can at worst leave a selection un-escalated — it can never corrupt a document. - Leave a classifier and instrumentation surface that Phase C builds on directly. **Non-Goals:** - No modification of transaction *changes* (no deletion rewriting, paste re-indentation, or vetoing) — that is Phase C. - No visual node-selection chrome beyond CM6's native selection rendering (a later polish layer; the decoration follow-ups parking lot holds related ideas). - No vim-mode support (Q8) and no mobile-specific testing (Q7: mobile-safe by construction, desktop-tested). - No changes to existing specs' behavior: grammar, structural commands, decorations, and off-mode behavior are untouched. ## Decisions ### D1. Mechanism: `EditorState.transactionFilter`, default-permit `transactionFilter` (not `changeFilter`) because Phase B must be able to replace the selection of a transaction, which `changeFilter` cannot. The filter is registered through the existing `registerEditorExtension` bundle in `main.ts` and gates itself per transaction: outline mode off (via the public `editorInfoField`, the same gate every other extension uses) → return the transaction unchanged immediately. Posture: **default-permit**. The classifier assigns exactly one class per transaction; any transaction that doesn't confidently match an enforced class passes through untouched. This is the safe polarity for an observer + selection-only change (worst case: missed escalation). Phase C will need default-deny reasoning for edits; choosing that polarity *now*, without Phase A's data, is exactly what this change exists to avoid. *Alternative considered*: `view.inputHandler`/DOM event handlers (obsidian-outliner's approach) — rejected; that is precisely the enumerate-the-inputs architecture the manifest rejects. ### D2. A pure classifier module, filter as thin adapter Classification logic lives in `src/` as a pure function over plain data — `classify(factsOfTransaction, tree) → class` — property-tested without Obsidian, in the same discipline as `ops.ts`/`dispatch.ts`. The CM6 adapter in `src/plugin/` only extracts facts (userEvent annotation, composition flag, change ranges as line spans, selection ranges) and applies the verdict. Phase C later swaps "verdict is a label" for "verdict may carry a rewrite" without re-architecting. The classes (exactly one per transaction, checked in this order): 1. `programmatic` — no `userEvent` annotation, or a remote/sync/undo-redo signature. Includes full-document `setValue`-style loads. Always pass through. 2. `composition` — IME composition in progress. Always pass through. 3. `plugin-own` — carries one of our grammar/command `userEvent` values; already valid by construction. Pass through. 4. `selection-only` — no doc changes; user selection event. Phase B's input. 5. `within-node-edit` — all change ranges fall inside single nodes' spans. Pass through. 6. `boundary-crossing-edit` — some change range crosses a node boundary. **In this change: pass through, but counted** — the count is the headline Phase A metric and the sizing input for Phase C. ### D3. Provenance via `userEvent` annotations, verified not assumed User-originated transactions are recognized by CM6's `Transaction.userEvent` annotation (`select`, `select.pointer`, `input.type`, `delete.*`, `move.drop`, `undo`/`redo`, …); absence of the annotation marks programmatic dispatches (plugins, Obsidian internals, sync/external reloads). This convention is load-bearing and is treated as a **hypothesis Phase A must confirm per source**: the e2e evidence suite drives each mutation path (typing, paste, mouse drag, find-and-replace, a programmatic `Vault.process`-style edit, an external-file reload) and asserts the observed classification. If a path arrives without a distinguishing annotation, the conservative default (pass through) already protects correctness; the finding gets recorded and the classifier tightened. Undo/redo pass through as programmatic-like (`undo`/`redo` userEvent): history transactions restore previously-accepted states and must never be re-normalized (Q11: CM6-native undo). ### D4. Selection escalation: minimal contiguous whole-subtree cover For each non-empty selection range in a `selection-only` transaction: - Resolve the lines of `anchor` and `head` to nodes (`nodeAtLine`). - If both ends resolve to the **same node** (including its trailing gap), the range is within-node: **untouched**. Word/phrase selection inside a node's text stays native. - Otherwise escalate: find the deepest common ancestor scope, take the two children-of-that-scope subtrees containing each end, and expand the range to cover the **contiguous run of whole sibling subtrees** between them (first subtree's first line, char 0 → last subtree's last line, end; trailing gap lines excluded from the visual selection but owned for Phase C semantics). - Anchor/head **orientation is preserved** (backward drags stay backward); only the positions move outward. Why subtrees and not bare nodes: a selection covering a heading but not its section (or a list item but not its children) has no valid structural meaning — every later operation on a node selection (delete, move, copy) targets subtrees, and Phase C's "deletion of an escalated selection is always a valid structural deletion" depends on this choice. This matches Workflowy/Logseq behavior when selection leaves a single item. Multi-range (multi-cursor) selections: each range escalates independently; `EditorSelection` normalization merges any resulting overlaps. Mechanics: the filter returns `[tr, { selection }]` so escalation lands **inside the same transaction** — no second dispatch, no history entry (selection-only transactions aren't history events), no observable intermediate state. ### D5. Jurisdiction: preamble and whole-document selections pass through `nodeAtLine` resolves frontmatter/preamble lines to nothing — they are outside the tree. Any selection range with an end in the preamble passes through unmodified, which also keeps Select All (Cmd+A) byte-for-byte native: it spans preamble + all nodes, so it is out of jurisdiction by this rule (and would escalate to itself anyway). Simple, predictable, and preserves the most muscle-memory-critical selection gesture. ### D6. Nested-editor safety: degeneracy by construction, verified, plus a flag if needed The decorations' `isNestedEditor()` DOM-ancestry gate is unavailable here — `transactionFilter` runs at state level with no view access. Primary strategy: **degeneracy by construction**. A nested per-cell editor's document is the cell's text — a single block, parsing to at most one node — so `within-node-edit` and same-node selection cover everything and the filter never acts. Phase A's e2e evidence must confirm this on the wide-table fixture (type, select, drag inside a cell), including the multi-block-cell edge (`
`-separated cell content) if reachable. Fallback (only if verification falsifies degeneracy): a `StateField` "nested" flag, false by default, set once via a `StateEffect` dispatched by a tiny `ViewPlugin` that *does* have DOM access — the same split the decorations use, adapted to state. The race window before the effect lands is covered by default-permit. This is designed now so a falsified hypothesis doesn't stall the change, but not built speculatively. ### D7. Tree access: parse cache keyed by document identity The filter needs the parsed tree for the transaction's document. Decision: a module-level `WeakMap` cache keyed by the CM6 `Text` instance — `parsedDoc(state.doc)` parses once per document version and is shared by every transaction (and reusable by any other consumer later). No new `StateField`, so no extension-ordering coupling inside the filter, and selection-only transactions (doc unchanged) always hit the cache. Performance budget (Phase A measures against it, via the observer's own timing counters): classification ≤ 1 ms median / ≤ 8 ms p95 per transaction on a ~2000-line stress note, on CI-class hardware. If a full re-parse per doc version misses the budget on edits, the fallback is incremental reuse (parse only from the first changed line's enclosing top-level block), designed but not built until the numbers demand it. ### D8. Observability: counters on the plugin, logging behind the existing debug setting A module-level stats object (per-class counts, per-class max/percentile timings, last N classified transactions in a ring buffer) exposed as a public field on the plugin instance — the e2e harness already executes JS in the app and reads plugin state the same way (`isOutlineMode`). Per-transaction console logging sits behind the existing crosscheck debug setting (same setting, same "developer console" contract — one debug switch, not a second one), plus a dev command that prints a stats summary. The stats surface is what turns each Phase A assumption into a permanent, assertable regression test rather than a one-off manual observation. ## Risks / Trade-offs - **[Escalation UX aggressiveness]** Shift+Down from mid-node into the next node jumps the selection to two whole subtrees — correct per the invariant, but potentially surprising mid-muscle-memory. → It is the manifest's defining behavior; mitigate with the real-vault manual pass judging feel, not just correctness. If it grates, a "boundary-clamp instead of escalate" alternative is a one-function swap in the pure module — record the verdict, don't pre-build both. - **[Live drag re-escalation]** CM6 re-derives pointer selections from the drag anchor each move; escalation re-applies per `select.pointer` transaction. Expected stable, but jitter (selection bouncing between native and escalated) would be disqualifying. → Explicit e2e scenario with a real mouse drag; if unstable, escalate only on drag end (pointer-up transaction) as the fallback. - **[userEvent taxonomy is convention, not contract]** Obsidian or third-party plugins may dispatch user-like transactions without annotations (classified `programmatic`, passed through) — an enforcement *gap*, not corruption, thanks to default-permit. → Phase A records observed annotations per source in the change docs; tighten classifier as evidence dictates. - **[Filter ordering vs other plugins]** Other plugins' transactionFilters/extenders may run after ours and produce a selection we never saw. Accepted for this change (coexistence warning already exists for outliner-class plugins, Q5); revisit with `Prec` only on observed conflict. - **[Parse cost on huge notes]** Full parse per doc version could blow the budget on 10k-line notes. → Budget + measurement are Phase A deliverables; incremental-reuse fallback designed (D7). A blown budget is a *finding*, not a silent ship. - **[Nested-editor degeneracy could be false]** If a table cell's mini-document can parse as multiple nodes, selection escalation could visibly corrupt cell editing — the exact bug class decorations hit. → Dedicated e2e on the wide-table fixture before Phase B is enabled anywhere; D6 fallback flag ready. ## Migration Plan Purely additive extension registration; no file-format, settings-schema, or command changes. Rollback = unregister the extension (revert the `main.ts` wiring); documents are untouched by construction (Phase A never alters text; Phase B alters only selection). Phase A findings that falsify an assumption get recorded in this design doc and docs/research/04 before Phase B ships, per the experiment-series discipline. ## Open Questions - Which exact `userEvent` values (if any) do Obsidian Sync and external-file reloads carry? (Phase A evidence suite answers; classifier ships conservative.) - Does live-drag escalation feel right, or should escalation land on pointer-up only? (Real-vault manual pass answers.) - Real per-transaction parse cost distribution at real note sizes — is incremental reuse needed at all? (Observer timing counters answer.)

# transaction-classification Delta

## ADDED Requirements

### Requirement: Enforcement funnel is registered and scoped to outline mode
A CM6 `transactionFilter` SHALL be registered via `registerEditorExtension` and SHALL
inspect every transaction dispatched in any editor. For editors whose file does not have
outline mode enabled (resolved through the public `editorInfoField`), the filter SHALL
return the transaction unchanged with no other observable effect, so off-mode editor
behavior is byte-for-byte and timing-indistinguishable from stock Obsidian.

#### Scenario: Off-mode transaction untouched
- **WHEN** any edit or selection change is made in a note without outline mode
- **THEN** the dispatched transaction is applied exactly as produced, and no
  classification side effects (stats, logs) are recorded for it

#### Scenario: Mode toggle takes effect immediately
- **WHEN** outline mode is toggled on for the active note
- **THEN** the very next transaction in that editor is classified, with no editor reload

### Requirement: Every transaction receives exactly one classification
In outline-mode editors the filter SHALL assign each transaction exactly one class from:
`programmatic`, `composition`, `plugin-own`, `selection-only`, `within-node-edit`,
`boundary-crossing-edit` — evaluated in that order, first match wins. Classification
SHALL be computed by a pure function over transaction facts and the parsed tree, unit-
and property-tested independently of Obsidian. Any transaction not confidently matching
an enforced class SHALL pass through unmodified (default-permit).

#### Scenario: Typing inside a node
- **WHEN** the user types a character in the middle of a paragraph node's text
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `within-node-edit` and applied unmodified

#### Scenario: Edit spanning two nodes counted but not altered
- **WHEN** a deletion's change range starts inside one node and ends inside the next
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `boundary-crossing-edit`, counted in the stats
  surface, and applied unmodified (rewriting is out of scope for this change)

### Requirement: Document text is never modified by this layer
The classification layer SHALL NOT add, remove, or alter any change spec of any
transaction, in any class, under any condition. Its only permitted transaction
modification is the selection replacement defined by the node-selection-enforcement
capability.

#### Scenario: Byte-identical buffer across classified edits
- **WHEN** any sequence of edits is dispatched in an outline-mode note with the filter
  active
- **THEN** the resulting buffer is byte-identical to the same sequence applied with the
  filter absent

### Requirement: Programmatic and remote transactions pass through untouched
Transactions carrying no `userEvent` annotation, or carrying undo/redo history
signatures, SHALL be classified `programmatic` and passed through with changes and
selection untouched. This SHALL hold for full-document loads and sync/external-reload
style replacements, preserving the interop guarantee that other tools' edits are never
fought or rewritten.

#### Scenario: External-style full-document replacement
- **WHEN** the document is replaced programmatically (a `setValue`-style dispatch with
  no user event, as an external reload or sync would produce)
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `programmatic` and applied byte-identically,
  including its selection

#### Scenario: Undo restores state without re-normalization
- **WHEN** the user invokes undo after any classified transaction
- **THEN** the history transaction passes through unmodified and restores the prior
  state exactly

### Requirement: Grammar and command transactions are recognized as plugin-own
Transactions dispatched by this plugin's own keyboard grammar and structural commands
(identified by their `userEvent` annotations) SHALL be classified `plugin-own` and
passed through unmodified — they are valid by construction and MUST NOT be re-processed.

#### Scenario: Tab indent is not reclassified
- **WHEN** Tab indents a node via the outline keyboard grammar
- **THEN** the resulting transaction is classified `plugin-own` and applied exactly as
  the grammar produced it, including its cursor placement

### Requirement: IME composition is never interfered with
Transactions that are part of an active IME composition SHALL be classified
`composition` and passed through with changes and selection untouched, regardless of
what spans they touch.

#### Scenario: Composition across a node boundary region
- **WHEN** an IME composition session is active in an outline-mode note
- **THEN** every composition transaction applies unmodified and composition completes
  exactly as in stock Obsidian

### Requirement: Nested editors are safe from enforcement effects
Classification and any enforcement built on it SHALL produce no observable effect
inside Obsidian's nested per-cell editors (e.g. Live Preview table cells), whose
transactions also flow through globally registered extensions. Safety SHALL be
verified against the existing wide-table fixture, not assumed.

#### Scenario: Editing a table cell in an outline-mode note
- **WHEN** the user types, selects, and drag-selects inside an actively edited table
  cell of an outline-mode note
- **THEN** cell content and cell selection behave byte-for-byte as stock Obsidian, with
  no escalation or other enforcement effect inside the cell

### Requirement: Classification meets the keystroke-latency budget
Per-transaction classification cost (including tree access) SHALL stay within the
budget of ≤ 1 ms median and ≤ 8 ms p95 on a ~2000-line stress note, measured by the
instrumentation's own timing counters, with the measured evidence recorded in the
change's verification notes. Parsed-tree access SHALL be cached per document version so
selection-only transactions never re-parse.

#### Scenario: Stress-note measurement recorded
- **WHEN** the evidence suite drives typing and selection across a ~2000-line note
- **THEN** recorded timings satisfy the budget, and the numbers (median/p95) are
  written into the change documentation

### Requirement: Classification is observable for verification
The plugin SHALL maintain a classification stats surface (per-class counters, timing
aggregates, and a bounded ring buffer of recent classifications) readable by automated
tests through the plugin instance. Per-transaction console logging SHALL be gated
behind the existing parser-crosscheck debug setting, and a developer command SHALL
print a stats summary on demand. Every mutation path in the coverage matrix — typing,
paste, mouse drag selection, keyboard selection, find-and-replace, drag-drop text,
programmatic edit — SHALL have an automated scenario asserting its observed
classification, forming the permanent evidence that all mutation paths flow through
the funnel.

#### Scenario: Paste is observed by the funnel
- **WHEN** multi-line text is pasted into an outline-mode note
- **THEN** the stats surface records a classification for the paste transaction

#### Scenario: Debug logging stays opt-in
- **WHEN** the debug setting is off
- **THEN** no per-transaction console output is produced, while counters still update

# node-selection-enforcement Delta

## ADDED Requirements

### Requirement: Boundary-crossing selections escalate to whole sibling subtrees
In outline mode, when a `selection-only` transaction contains a non-empty range whose
anchor and head resolve to different nodes of the parsed tree, the filter SHALL replace
that range with the minimal contiguous cover of whole sibling subtrees: the run of
children of the ends' deepest common ancestor scope that spans both ends, from the first
subtree's first character to the last subtree's last character. A node is never
partially selected together with content outside it.

#### Scenario: Drag from mid-paragraph into the next paragraph
- **WHEN** the user drag-selects from the middle of one paragraph node into the middle
  of the next sibling paragraph
- **THEN** the selection becomes both paragraphs in full

#### Scenario: Selection leaving a parent covers its subtree
- **WHEN** a selection starts inside a heading's text and ends inside a paragraph
  within that heading's section
- **THEN** the selection covers the heading's entire subtree (the heading line and all
  nodes in its section)

#### Scenario: Keyboard selection crossing a boundary
- **WHEN** the user extends a selection with Shift+ArrowDown from inside one node into
  the next node
- **THEN** the resulting selection covers both nodes' subtrees in full

### Requirement: Within-node selections and cursors are untouched
Selection ranges whose two ends resolve to the same node SHALL pass through unmodified,
and empty ranges (cursors) SHALL never be moved by this layer, so all within-node text
selection (words, phrases, partial lines) and cursor placement remain byte-for-byte
native.

#### Scenario: Double-click word selection
- **WHEN** the user double-clicks a word inside a node
- **THEN** the native word selection is applied unmodified

#### Scenario: Cursor placement is never escalated
- **WHEN** the user clicks to place the cursor anywhere in an outline-mode note
- **THEN** the cursor lands exactly where stock Obsidian would place it

### Requirement: Escalation preserves orientation and transaction integrity
An escalated range SHALL preserve the original anchor/head orientation (backward
selections stay backward), and the replacement SHALL occur within the same transaction
via the filter's return value — no additional dispatch, no history entry, and no
observable intermediate selection state.

#### Scenario: Backward drag stays backward
- **WHEN** the user drags a selection upward from a lower node into an upper node
- **THEN** the escalated selection covers both subtrees with the head at the start side

#### Scenario: Escalation during live drag is stable
- **WHEN** the user drags across a node boundary and continues dragging
- **THEN** each pointer update yields the escalated selection without flicker between
  native and escalated states

### Requirement: Multi-range selections escalate per range
For multi-cursor/multi-range selections, each non-empty range SHALL be escalated
independently under the same rules, with standard `EditorSelection` normalization
merging any overlapping results.

#### Scenario: Two ranges, one crossing a boundary
- **WHEN** a multi-range selection has one within-node range and one boundary-crossing
  range
- **THEN** the within-node range is untouched and the crossing range escalates

### Requirement: Preamble and out-of-jurisdiction selections pass through
Selection ranges with either end in the document preamble (frontmatter or other content
before the first node) SHALL pass through unmodified. Select All SHALL behave exactly as
stock Obsidian.

#### Scenario: Select All is native
- **WHEN** the user presses the Select All shortcut in an outline-mode note with
  frontmatter
- **THEN** the entire document is selected exactly as in stock Obsidian

### Requirement: Enforcement is scoped to outline mode and enforced classes only
Selection escalation SHALL apply only in outline-mode editors and only to transactions
classified `selection-only`. Transactions of every other class — including
`programmatic`, `composition`, and `plugin-own` — SHALL keep their selections untouched,
and off-mode notes SHALL show byte-for-byte stock selection behavior.

#### Scenario: Off-mode drag selection is native
- **WHEN** the user drag-selects across paragraphs in a note without outline mode
- **THEN** the selection is exactly the native character-level selection

#### Scenario: Programmatic selection restore is untouched
- **WHEN** a transaction without a user event restores a mid-node selection (e.g.
  workspace restore)
- **THEN** the selection is applied exactly as dispatched, even if it crosses node
  boundaries

All four artifacts are created and the change validates. Here's what's in place:

**Change: `outline-selection-enforcement`** at [openspec/changes/outline-selection-enforcement/](openspec/changes/outline-selection-enforcement/)

- **[proposal.md](openspec/changes/outline-selection-enforcement/proposal.md)** — why the enforcement funnel is the next core bet to validate; Phase A (observe/classify) + Phase B (selection escalation), with Phase C edit rewriting explicitly out of scope. Two new capabilities, no modifications to existing specs.
- **[design.md](openspec/changes/outline-selection-enforcement/design.md)** — the load-bearing decisions: `transactionFilter` with a **default-permit** posture (a misclassification in this change can at worst miss an escalation, never corrupt text); a pure classifier module with a six-class taxonomy (`programmatic`/`composition`/`plugin-own`/`selection-only`/`within-node-edit`/`boundary-crossing-edit`); escalation to whole **sibling subtrees** (not bare nodes) so Phase C's deletion semantics are valid by construction; nested-editor safety via the degeneracy hypothesis (a cell's mini-document parses as one node → the filter never acts) with a designed-but-not-built StateField fallback since the decorations' DOM-ancestry gate is unavailable at state level; a WeakMap parse cache with a concrete perf budget (≤1 ms median / ≤8 ms p95 on a ~2000-line note); stats exposed on the plugin instance, logging behind the existing crosscheck debug setting.
- **Specs** — [transaction-classification](openspec/changes/outline-selection-enforcement/specs/transaction-classification/spec.md) (9 requirements: scoping, one-class-per-transaction, the never-modify-text contract, programmatic/undo pass-through, plugin-own recognition, IME, nested editors, perf budget, observability) and [node-selection-enforcement](openspec/changes/outline-selection-enforcement/specs/node-selection-enforcement/spec.md) (6 requirements: subtree escalation, within-node/cursor untouched, orientation + same-transaction integrity, multi-range, preamble/Select All jurisdiction, off-mode/class scoping).
- **[tasks.md](openspec/changes/outline-selection-enforcement/tasks.md)** — 6 groups, 26 tasks, ordered so the Phase A evidence suite (group 3) is a hard gate: task 3.8 explicitly blocks Phase B if any choke-point assumption is falsified, keeping the experiment-series discipline.

One design judgment worth your eyes before implementation: escalation covers whole *subtrees* (selecting from a heading into its section grabs the entire section, Workflowy-style). It's the structurally correct choice, but it's also the most aggressive UX in the change — the real-vault manual pass (task 6.2) is scoped to judge exactly that, with a boundary-clamp alternative noted as a one-function swap if it grates.

Ready for implementation — run `/opsx:apply` or ask me to start on the tasks.
